did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used an ensemble of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) trained on the HAM10000 dataset to classify seven types of skin cancer (actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, dermatofibroma, melanoma, nevus, vascular lesion, and benign keratosis-like lesions). The study employed deep learning architectures with specific layers (Conv2D, MaxPool2D, etc.) and optimizers (Adam, RMSProp, Adadelta) to achieve a classification accuracy of 92.67%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of an ensemble CNN system for skin cancer classification using the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific methodology (data preprocessing, model training with Adam, RMSProp, Adadelta optimizers), results (92.67% accuracy, confusion matrix, per-class metrics), and a conclusion based on these new findings. Although published in a conference proceedings (HNICEM), it reports original technical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Cancer Classification using Ensemble CNN
journal: 2024 IEEE 16th International Conference on Humanoid, Nanotechnology, Information Technology, Communication and Control, Environment, and Management (HNICEM)
brief_summary: This study developed an ensemble of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to classify seven types of skin cancer: actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, dermatofibroma, melanoma, nevus, vascular lesion, and benign keratosis-like lesions. The researchers utilized the publicly available HAM10000 dataset for training and evaluation, employing custom CNN architectures with Adam, RMSprop, and Adadelta optimizers. The effectiveness of the system was assessed using a confusion matrix to calculate per-class accuracy, precision, and recall rates. The ensemble approach achieved an overall classification accuracy of 92.67%, with the Adam optimizer demonstrating the most stable performance in terms of loss reduction. The results indicate that ensemble learning with CNNs is a viable method for accurate skin cancer detection and differentiation.

all_task_types.target: Seven discrete types of skin cancer (actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, dermatofibroma, melanoma, nevus, vascular lesion, and benign keratosis-like lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'Skin Cancer Classification' using an ensemble of CNNs. The model predicts one of seven discrete diagnostic categories from the HAM10000 dataset. Performance is evaluated using a confusion matrix, accuracy (92.67%), precision, and recall. While confidence scores are output, they are used for class prediction, not as a continuous regression target.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset of skin cancer images used for training and testing the ensemble CNN models. The dataset contains images of seven types of skin cancer.

use.training: Used to train the CNN models with Adam, RMSProp, and Adadelta optimizers.
use.validation: Used to monitor validation accuracy and loss during training to assess model performance and generalization.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final system's effectiveness via a confusion matrix, calculating per-class accuracy, precision, and recall. A subset of 11 samples was used for GUI application testing.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To measure the trained model's accuracy, precision, and recall rate for each skin cancer class by comparing predicted values against actual values.

statistical_methods_used.method: Per-class Accuracy, Precision, and Recall Rate Calculation
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the performance of the model for each specific class (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, NV, VASC, MEL) using specific equations derived from the confusion matrix.
To balance the class distribution of the training dataset by creating synthetic sample images for classes with low sample counts, addressing class imbalance.
To monitor model performance during training and validation epochs for Adam, RMSProp, and Adadelta optimizers to assess convergence and stability.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and loss metrics derived from a confusion matrix.

HAM10000.experiment: Ensemble CNN with Adam, RMSProp, and Adadelta Optimizers

Adam.notes: Showed rapid convergence and stable validation accuracy. Best and most stable performance.

RMSProp.notes: Validation accuracy varied more, suggesting potential overfitting. More fluctuating loss pattern.

Adadelta.notes: Exhibited slower but steady progress. Limited validation performance.

HAM10000.notes: The Adam optimizer demonstrated the most stable performance. The ensemble approach achieved an overall accuracy of 92.67%. Class imbalance was addressed using RandomOverSampler.

training_details.epochs: Not explicitly reported (Accuracy vs Epoch graph referenced)

preprocessing_techniques: Class balancing using RandomOverSampler from the imblearn library to create synthetic sample images for classes with low sample counts
Dataset splitting into 80% training and 20% testing sets

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used from the imblearn library to balance class distribution by creating synthetic sample images for classes with low sample counts.

conclusions: An ensemble of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) using Adam, RMSProp, and Adadelta optimizers can effectively classify seven types of skin cancer (actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, dermatofibroma, melanoma, nevus, vascular lesion, and benign keratosis-like lesions) with an overall accuracy of 92.67%.

The Adam optimizer demonstrated the best and most stable performance among the tested optimizers, achieving an accuracy of 92.96% and consistent loss reduction (training loss from 0.45 to 0.18, validation loss from 0.50 to 0.20).
The RMSProp optimizer achieved 81.23% accuracy but showed potential overfitting with more fluctuating validation accuracy and loss patterns.
The Adadelta optimizer achieved 86.37% accuracy but exhibited limited validation performance, with validation accuracy plateauing at 58.60% despite reaching 95% training accuracy.
Class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset was addressed using RandomOverSampler to create synthetic sample images for classes with low sample counts.

Data preprocessing techniques, including Contrast-Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) and Multiscale Retinex Color Restoration (MSRCR), were utilized to enhance image quality and classification accuracy.

The study acknowledges that previous research often relied on limited datasets or imbalanced classes, and that using pretrained models may not always yield the best architecture for skin cancer detection.
The developed system provides an initial identification of skin cancer types within a short span of time, aiding dermatologists in early detection and diagnosis.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges that previous research often relied on limited datasets or imbalanced classes, creating bias, and that using pretrained models may not always yield the best architecture for skin cancer detection.

The paper critiques the optimism of accuracy rates exceeding 95% with small, unevenly divided training sets (e.g., 250 images across 3-8 classes), suggesting such results are unrealistic for skin cancer classification.

The dataset (HAM10000) exhibits significant class imbalance, with Nevus (NV) having 1313 samples compared to Dermatofibroma (DF) with only 25, necessitating the use of RandomOverSampler to create synthetic samples.
The model was trained on a custom CNN architecture rather than using transfer learning with pre-trained models like VGG16 or ResNet, which are commonly used in prior studies.

The Adam optimizer demonstrated the most stable performance with rapid convergence, while RMSProp showed potential overfitting and Adadelta had limited validation performance despite high training accuracy.
The study is limited to seven specific types of skin cancer and uses a publicly available dataset, which may limit the generalizability of the results to other populations or clinical settings.
The ensemble learning approach achieved an overall accuracy of 92.67%, with the Adam optimizer achieving the highest individual accuracy of 92.96%.
The system was tested using a GUI application with a small subset of 11 samples, where false positives were detected in samples 2, 12, and 13.
